review_summary: This unit is suitable for questions on interpreting attention maps and abstracting attention as a query-to-data operator. The text provides enough detail to assess diagonal alignments, reordering, and the general attention pipeline.

question_bank.question: In a translation attention heatmap, what does a mostly diagonal pattern usually indicate?
choices: Input and output tokens are largely aligned in the same order.
The model has ignored the input sequence entirely.
Every output token depends equally on every input token.
The decoder is using only the final encoder hidden state.
question_bank.explanation: A diagonal attention pattern means that early output tokens attend to early input tokens, middle to middle, and so on. That is the visual signature of mostly in-order alignment.

question_bank.question: What does an off-diagonal or blocky region in an attention map often suggest?
choices: The model has learned a reordering or phrase-level alignment rather than a simple one-to-one mapping.
The attention weights failed to pass through softmax.
The input and output sequences must have identical lengths.
The model is computing a convolution instead of attention.
question_bank.explanation: Attention maps can reveal alignments that are not one-to-one or strictly ordered. Blocks and off-diagonal regions often arise when phrases reorder or multiple words correspond across languages.

question_bank.question: Abstractly, what does an attention operator do for each query vector?
choices: It compares the query with data vectors and returns a query-specific summary of those data vectors.
It sorts data vectors and returns only the first one in the sorted list.
It deletes all data vectors that are not adjacent to the query.
It converts every query into a fixed one-hot class label.
question_bank.explanation: Attention is a content-based retrieval operation. A query determines which data vectors matter, and the output is a weighted summary tailored to that query.
